description: This figure presents two scatter plots showing the room-temperature mechanical properties of lightweight Fe–Mn–Al–C steels as functions of Mn and C content. Panel (a) displays yield strength (YS, hollow symbols) and ultimate tensile strength (UTS, solid symbols) versus elongation for γ-based duplex (γ+α/δ) steels with average Mn contents (8–12 wt%) and varying carbon levels (0.5–1.2 wt%), where data points cluster at 600–1400 MPa strength with 5–40% elongation. Panel (b) expands the composition range to high Mn contents (12–40 wt%) across four Mn intervals, revealing two distinct performance envelopes: the γ-based duplex regime (blue shaded area, ~600–1200 MPa, 10–60% elongation) and the single-γ austenitic regime (grey shaded area, ~800–2000 MPa, 40–90% elongation). The charts demonstrate that increasing Mn content stabilizes the austenite phase, transitioning the alloy from duplex to single-phase microstructure, which generally increases ductility but with varied strength levels depending on carbon content. The systematic variation of symbol types (representing 0.5–>1.2 wt% C) and colors (representing different Mn ranges) illustrates the compositional dependence of the strength-ductility balance, supporting the conclusion that Mn and C concentrations critically determine phase stability and mechanical property optimization in lightweight steels.
